does: This paper introduces MedPRMBench, the first process-level reward model benchmark specifically designed for medical reasoning. The authors develop a three-phase pipeline grounded in Clinical Reasoning Blueprints to systematically generate high-quality evaluation data from seven medical QA sources. The benchmark encompasses 6,500 questions with 13,000 reasoning chains and over 113,000 step-level labels, categorizing errors into 14 fine-grained types across three dimensions: Simplicity, Soundness, and Sensitivity. Notably, MedPRMBench introduces the first 4-level severity grading system tailored to quantify clinical impact, distinguishing medical applications from general domains. The benchmark enables precise evaluation of how well process reward models can detect errors at individual reasoning steps in medical problem-solving, addressing the gap between general-domain PRM capabilities and domain-specific safety requirements in healthcare.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature of process reward models, which have emerged as crucial components for improving complex reasoning in large language models beyond standard outcome-based feedback. While prior PRM research has focused on mathematical reasoning and general domains, this paper responds to the critical absence of medical-domain evaluation frameworks. It extends reward modeling research by introducing domain-specific error taxonomy and severity grading informed by clinical practice standards. The work connects to broader efforts in LLM evaluation for high-stakes domains and safety-critical applications, complementing existing outcome-level medical QA benchmarks that cannot assess intermediate reasoning quality. By building on Clinical Reasoning Blueprints as a structural foundation, it incorporates medical domain expertise into benchmark construction, contrasting with purely data-driven approaches used in general-domain PRM benchmarks.
enables: This benchmark enables future research in developing and evaluating process reward models specifically designed for medical applications, where step-level reasoning accuracy directly impacts patient safety. The 4-level severity grading system creates opportunities for training reward models that can prioritize clinically consequential errors over minor mistakes. The fine-grained error categorization across Simplicity, Soundness, and Sensitivity dimensions allows researchers to analyze failure modes systematically and develop targeted improvements for medical LLMs. The work supports development of more reliable decision-support systems and opens pathways for using process rewards to improve medical reasoning chains through reinforcement learning approaches tailored to healthcare. Additionally, it provides a foundation for extending process reward modeling to other safety-critical domains beyond medicine that share similar requirements for knowledge intensity and error criticality.
